speaker: interviewer
text: You were talking about the close calls you had in the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Was there something else you wanted to say about that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there was one time-- I would dig myself, like, a little grave. And that's where I was hiding. I mean, that, that was my place to survive, to live. And one time, I was in my little hole. And all of a sudden, I saw a pair of boots in front of me. And that was the [NON-ENGLISH]. This was, like, the park attendant-- what do you call them--

speaker: interviewer
text: The ranger?

speaker: interviewee
text: The ranger, yes. And there he was, looking down at me. And he says, who are you? What are you doing here? And I mean, I did not-- I said, I'm hiding. And he says, well, you'll never survive. Winter is coming. Let me take you to the authorities and put you out of your misery. And I said, well, no. I'll take my chances. Don't denounce me. Don't report me. And he says, well, I'll be doing you a favor. You can't survive here. And I took out a coin from my coat, which I still had with me. And he walked away. I mean-- and then, several weeks later, he must have watched me because he ended up in another place. Because he knew where I had the money, you know, the coins. And I gave him another coin. And he let me go. The, the way I make my way in, in, in the forest, someone asked me before. And again, I don't know how to intelligently tell you because I didn't have any plan. I didn't know-- I finally learned where was, you know, north, south, and east and west because of the sun and of the moss, you know, on the trees. But I didn't know any of that. I think what really helped me a great deal-- I mentioned before, I was a tomboy. And I-- for my fourth or fifth birthday, I'm not quite sure, I asked my parents to give me a penknife, a little knife. And there was a lot of debate and a hesitation. But they did give me one. And I've never been without a little knife since, even now.

speaker: interviewer
text: You had that knife with you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I did. Yes, I did. And that knife came in very handy and, and saved me in many situations, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: How?

speaker: interviewee
text: I killed birds. I dug my little holes, you know, because I didn't have anything else. I would cut some vegetables and all if I would sneak into some gardens. Oh, there were countless ways in which my little knife came in handy. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I, I, I, I can't think of-- but there were so-- so many ways. I, I, I mean, for me to get some food and all was the main objective in life because even though I still kept thinking of-- I kept looking for my mother. I think, somehow deep within me, I knew that I will not find her. But I, I, I kept deluding myself. And even though I kept looking for her, my main objective was survival. And, at that point, winter set in, and it was very difficult to exist from day to day. The cold was absolutely impossible to describe. And, in addition to everything else, the loneliness was awful. I don't know how to describe to you a feeling of being the only human being and not hearing another human voice for weeks at the time. It got so bad that, at times, I would sneak into a village and sneak into a barn just to hear another human voice. And yet, the longing for the human voice was not good for me because any human or anything alive was my enemy. Everything and anything was my enemy. So I was torn into two different directions. The way to survive in the wintertime was really a challenge beyond description. At-- by that time, everything was in shreds. I didn't have any shoes. And I would wrap some twigs with some pieces of, of, of rope that I would find. And that was my-- these were my shoes. I now have frostbitten toes. And, as matter of fact, I just had some surgery. And everything is fine. But it was very difficult. But somehow, the will to live was so strong in me that I kept going on. I ended up talking to myself. Animals would come to me in, in the forest. And, I don't know, perhaps it was that the smell was the same by that time-- I was filthy and smelly-- they would lick my face. A bear licked my face. I don't know if it was a very old, mama bear. Foxes would come and look, come close, and warm me. The only animal that wasn't friendly were dogs. Dogs-- you know, going into the villages, sneaking in for some kind of food, the dogs would bark and give me away. In the wintertime, it was very difficult because in addition to the dogs, you also had the snow-- the footprints in the snow. And that was very, very difficult. And consequently, I would hide wherever and go without food for days and sometimes weeks. I would eat anything and everything. I ate birds. I ate worms. I ate leaves. [CRYING] I ate my own feces. [SOB] I'm sorry. [CRYING] One time, I was in a village in a barn, and a young boy saw me. And again, he ran to announce to the whole world, there was a Jew. And I didn't know where to run. And I ran. And all of a sudden, there was a water well. And I jumped into the water well. And they looked all over for me. They didn't look in the water well. I spent several days in the water well. And somehow, they didn't discover me. But clawing my way out of that water well was awful. And you know, I would try-- the, the, the, the walls were slick and slippery. And I would try-- make one step up and two steps down. And it was wet in the water. Somehow, water and I do not get along, it seems like. But it took me days to claw my way out. But I did make it. And I don't know if it was an abandoned water well or what, because it wasn't used while I was there.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another time, I ran into a group of partisans. And that I really greeted with great hope. And I came before them and asked them, could I join them? And of course, the first question was, are you a Jew? And I said, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: And they said, well-- they didn't say they weren't going to accept me. They said, we have a policy. Anyone who joins us must contribute a weapon.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I took out my knife, my little knife. That was my weapon. And they laughed in my face. And they said I didn't have a weapon. I cannot join their group.

speaker: interviewee
text: They fed me. And I was to leave. And at night when everyone was asleep except some guards, I appropriated a pair of boots-- one was about two sizes bigger than the other-- and a warm shawl, and some food, and walked away. And these boots saved my life for the next winter. Every day was filled with uncertainty, with terror, hunger, and thirst. Thirst was a very important suffering for me too. I don't know how to describe it. But sometimes, thirst is worse than hunger. And I would drink my own urine. It, it, it-- it's, it's ironic, in a way. Now everyone is on diets. And nobody can imagine, really, what real thirst and what real hunger is. But I went through all that. And when I think now, I'm afraid of my own shadow. And when I see a fly, I wouldn't eat the bread or so if the fly was on it. It's curious. But in those days, it was just a matter of survival. And I did all these indescribable things to survive. But I am proud of one thing. I never killed another human being. And I don't think I'm capable of doing such a thing, regardless. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There were incidents daily. And I could probably go on for a long time. But every day was a fight for life.

speaker: interviewer
text: What kept you going?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think, deep in my heart, it was somehow trying to convince myself that I'll be back with my family again and also, a terrible feeling of guilt, of a sense of guilt, that I let my wonderful mother down. I had so much explaining to do to her-- and why I dozed off when I shouldn't have, why I didn't want to eat the bread that she insisted I eat. That was a feeling that, that kept me going. I sang to myself. I talked to myself. I probably even daydreamed. I don't know. In the spring, it was very interesting. I watched little birds hatch, you know, from eggs. And it was the miracle of life. And it gave me hope. As young as I was, I realized that that was a miracle of life. And I clung to it. I would never-- in the spring, I would never eat birds. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you sleep outdoors every night, or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Most of the nights, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you do that in the winter?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, in the winter, I would dig myself in, sometimes for a week or so, in the hole and cover myself with leaves and rags. And it was awful. It was. Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when did this go on until?

speaker: interviewee
text: This went on-- I kept saying that I was in the forest for three years, but no. I was only for two years in the forest, only for two years. But this went on until-- I recall I was-- I became very ill and very weak. And I was in the forest in my little grave. And I was so hungry and so tired and so weary that I, I thought it was going to be the end. And, in a way, part of me was relieved. No more worrying, no more being afraid. I'll find peace. But then, that will to live won over. And I started searching for food. And I walked and walked. And I knew that at that point, I could not make it to the nearest village. And so I ended up in a cellar where the farmers used to keep potatoes for the winter and other vegetables. And I looked around, hoping to find some potatoes or something like that, or carrots or cabbages, whatever. There was nothing left in that cellar. And there were some empty potato sacks. And I took a few to wrap myself in and climbed out of that cellar, trying to make my way back to the forest. And I was very, very weak and very, very sick. And I walked very slowly. And all of a sudden, I felt something soft in one of the potato sacks. And there was a-- it was a dead animal. It was a rat. [CRYING] I ripped part of it and ate it. And by that time, I was so ill and nauseous, I got confused. And I couldn't find the place where I was hiding in the forest. And so I just laid down and thought I was going to die. And I don't remember really what happened from then on. But I understand that the Russian soldiers found me. And they literally stepped on me and felt I was still alive. They cleaned me up. And they put me in some field hospitals and tents. And I was going from one to the other. I, I vaguely remember it, but I really don't.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: That was in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what time of year?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was winter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So towards the end of '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes. Yeah, mm-hmm. And, you see, the front was advancing. They were fighting and chasing away the Germans. And they took me with them. They were very kind to me. They cleaned me, and they fed me. And then finally, after a few weeks, they left me in a hospital in Lutsk, which was near my hometown. And there I came-- I mean, I regained my full consciousness. And I was in a regular hospital. And they cared for me. When they left me there, they left me with a little tag saying, 'This is a child of the forest. Care for her gently.' [CRYING] And they did. I did not speak for about a month. And one day, a young woman came in and was an attendant at a hospital. And she started humming a tune that was familiar to me. It was an old Polish folksong, a lullaby, actually, that my mother used to sing to me. And all of a sudden, I burst-- I mean, with a torrent of words-- and I started speaking again. It was very difficult in the beginning. And I was confused. And I really didn't know what was going on. I was very weak. I don't recall exactly how much longer I was in that hospital. But they did nurse me back to health. And that was the beginning of my physical healing. Emotionally, it took-- it's still going on. When I got well enough to leave the hospital, I still had one coin left. And I left it with the people at the hospital. I tried to trace and to find out who really gave me back the gift of life in the forest. I was never able to get names. But they told me it was a, a, a Russian division-- soldiers from a Russian division, two soldiers who found me. And they cared for me, and they are the ones responsible for my being alive today. When I got better, by that time, the war was still on, but we were liberated by the Soviet soldiers. At that time, there were a lot-- not a lot, but there were several other survivors in Lutsk. And we all got together, looking for our loved ones and trying to pick up life from the beginning. Each one of us had a different story. And yet they were all very similar. I decided to make my way back to my hometown, again, hoping against hope that I would find my family. Needless to say, I went back with the other survivor from my hometown, whom I met in Lutsk and who lives now in Duluth, Minnesota, and we went back to my hometown. And we didn't find anything or anybody. The place where the ghetto was, was completely leveled. Our house, my house, was still there. And of course, all the people around surrounded us. And they kept saying, oh, if only we knew what was going on, if only we knew, they would have helped, obviously. That time, I found out that my sister was denounced several days after she went into hiding. And she was paraded in the nude in the Main Street, tortured. And then they murdered her.

speaker: interviewer
text: In your-- in your home town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In my, in my, in my town, yes. Who, I don't know. Nobody-- well, I had two versions about my father. One version was that they took him in a group of the first-- the people whom they rounded up at first, that they took them to Dachau. And the other version was that they made them dig a big pit right outside our town. And they killed him right there and then. Nobody knew or knows anything about the fate of my mother. There is really no closure to my grief. To this day, when I walk and I see somebody walking in front of me who's very tall and reminds me of the build of my father-- and my mother was also almost six foot tall-- when I see people that remind me of my father and mother, I have to run in front and look in their faces to convince myself that it's not them. [SIGH] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I don't know how to express my feelings now. In the beginning, it was rage, anger, and hate, yeah. Now, I don't know what I would do if I would meet, person-to-person, the human being who murdered my father or my mother. I don't know if I am capable of avenging their death with, with the death of the perpetrator. But I cannot forget. And I cannot forgive, not yet.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you come to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I came to my hometown and I found not-- nobody, I went back to Lutsk. And there were other survivors like me. And we tried to comfort each other and see what else we could do. Well, at that time, people kept telling us, whoever survived is now in Germany, because everybody was concentrating in Germany. By that time, I guess the war was over. And we decided, we have to make our way to Germany. And so a group of us did that. And it was an, an illegal way of doing it. We went from our place, from our town, Horochow, back to Lutsk. From Lutsk, we went to Bratislava. That's in, in, in Czechoslovakia at the time. From Bratislava, we made our way to Vienna, Austria. And from Vienna, we ended up in Germany. Now, in Germany, they organized, in the beginning right after the war, displaced persons camps, DP camps. The only bad thing in the beginning was that everyone who made their way into Germany, and that included survivors like myself plus the people who were the collaborators with the Germans. They were also running. And so we all ended up in the same camps. I did not recognize anyone that I knew. But other survivors did. And consequently, there were-- you know, there were fights and rightly so. And eventually, they separated us. And so we ended up in different DP camps. I remembered, way, way back, the address of my grandmother. Don't ask me what I ate yesterday, but her address was 231 Echo Place, Bronx 57, New York. I remember that.

speaker: interviewee
text: I remember it because on one of my birthdays when I was four or five-- and there was talk about our going to America. That was very prevalent in my home. And I remember my father and I walked along the river, and I asked him, Papa, is this America on the other side? He says, no. That's Skobelka. But I mean, that's the way it was ingrained. And I said, well, when we get to America, will we visit with Bubby? And Bubby lived at 231 Echo Place.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, anyway, I remembered that address. And I wrote to that address. By that time, my grandmother, I'm told now, died literally of a broken heart when the war started, because her one and only beloved son never made it to America. But even though I remembered that and I had a connection, and my relatives in America were vouching that I would not become a burden to the state, I still had to wait three long years in different DP camps before they allowed me to emigrate to America. And during the three years, it was-- life in the, in the DP camps was not like in the forest. But I felt very much alone. I felt abandoned. I wasn't cared for. I mean, we had a shower once a week. We were-- we had rationed food, which was better than trying to, you know, find food for yourself. But it was a very primitive life. And I was very disappointed and very discouraged with my life after fighting for, for it, you know, for so long.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now, by this time, you were about 16 years old. Did you ever have the opportunity to have any fun, to regain any of that-- those kinds of experiences?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't think so. I don't think I ever knew how to relax, how to have fun. It was a life of turmoil. And the goal at that time was to get out of Germany. And I was very discouraged when I couldn't get to join my family in America. And I longed for it badly.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I decided-- in one of the DP camps, I decided to attend university. And that in itself was not stupid, but improbable. My formal education was [SIGHS] nonexistent, to speak-- I mean, nothing to speak of, what, third grade or second grade? OK, we have to change the tape once more. OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. All right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Sorry.
